puzzles.themeBlurb: A museum, a Trojan prince, a plaster cast, a Texas town, and a wine upset — every answer carries the same name.

questions.question: Which famous Leonardo da Vinci portrait hangs behind glass in the Louvre?
questions.connection: The Louvre — and the Mona Lisa — live in Paris.

questions.question: In Greek myth, which Trojan prince's abduction of Helen sparked the Trojan War?
questions.connection: Paris of Troy.

questions.question: What is the common name of the white powder (calcium sulfate hemihydrate) used to make casts and molds?
questions.connection: Plaster of Paris, named for the gypsum quarried under the city.

questions.question: Which Wim Wenders road movie won the Palme d'Or in 1984?
questions.connection: Paris, Texas — a small town sharing the famous name.

questions.question: What is the nickname of the famous 1976 blind wine tasting in which California wines beat the French?
questions.connection: The Judgment of Paris — itself named after the myth.

puzzles.themeBlurb: Ancient wonders, a gunslinger posse, Olympic rugby, a stadium riff, and Shakespeare's ages of man — count them.

questions.question: The Great Pyramid of Giza is the only surviving member of which famous ancient list?
options: The Seven Wonders of the World
accept: seven wonders of the ancient world
questions.connection: The Seven Wonders of the Ancient World.

questions.question: Which 1960 western is a remake of Akira Kurosawa's 'Seven Samurai'?
questions.connection: The Magnificent Seven.

questions.question: Which fast-paced rugby format became an Olympic sport at Rio 2016?
questions.connection: Rugby sevens — seven players a side.

questions.question: Which White Stripes song's bass-like guitar riff became a global stadium chant?
options: Fell in Love with a Girl
questions.connection: Seven Nation Army.

questions.question: The 'All the world's a stage' speech, describing the seven ages of man, appears in which Shakespeare play?
questions.connection: The speech walks through the seven ages of man.

puzzles.themeBlurb: Every answer traces back to one remarkable year: the Moon landing, Woodstock, Broadway Joe, the birth of the internet, and the Duke's only Oscar.

questions.question: Who was the first person to walk on the Moon?
questions.connection: Apollo 11 landed on July 20, 1969.

questions.question: Which legendary music festival took place on Max Yasgur's dairy farm in upstate New York?
questions.connection: Woodstock ran for three days in August 1969.

questions.question: Which quarterback famously guaranteed — and delivered — a Super Bowl III upset win for the New York Jets?
questions.connection: Super Bowl III was played in January 1969.

questions.question: What was the name of the pioneering network that sent its first message between UCLA and Stanford?
questions.connection: ARPANET's first message was sent on October 29, 1969.

questions.question: Which film earned John Wayne his only Academy Award for Best Actor?
questions.connection: True Grit was released in 1969.

puzzles.themeBlurb: From the Delta to deep space, every answer today wears the same color.

questions.question: Which genre, born in the American Deep South, gave rock and roll its 12-bar structure?
questions.connection: The blues — it's right there in the name.

questions.question: Lake Tana in Ethiopia is the source of which river?
questions.connection: The Blue Nile supplies most of the Nile's water.

questions.question: Which IBM computer defeated world chess champion Garry Kasparov in 1997?
questions.connection: Deep Blue was the first computer to beat a reigning world champion in a match.

questions.question: Which period of Picasso's career (1901–1904) was defined by somber, monochromatic paintings?
questions.connection: Picasso's Blue Period was painted almost entirely in shades of blue.

questions.question: What is the name of the famous 45.5-carat diamond housed at the Smithsonian Institution?
questions.connection: The Hope Diamond is famous for its deep blue color.

puzzles.themeBlurb: Rock royalty, a giant ape, a boy pharaoh, a master of horror, and the world's longest venomous snake — all crowned.

questions.question: Which 'King of Rock and Roll' recorded his first songs at Sun Studio in Memphis?
questions.connection: Elvis is 'The King.'

questions.question: Which giant ape first climbed the Empire State Building in a 1933 film?
questions.connection: King Kong — the king of Skull Island.

questions.question: Whose nearly intact tomb did archaeologist Howard Carter discover in 1922?
questions.connection: Tutankhamun is better known as King Tut.

questions.question: Which author's first published novel was 'Carrie' in 1974?
questions.connection: Stephen King, the king of horror.

questions.question: 'Ophiophagus hannah' is the scientific name of which animal?
questions.connection: The king cobra is the world's longest venomous snake.

puzzles.themeBlurb: Charisma, a cursed state, a background character, a toilet-headed army — and Thoreau, who was already complaining about brain rot in 1854. Oxford made it Word of the Year in 2024. The rot is older than the internet.

questions.question: Oxford named which word — short for "charisma" — its 2023 Word of the Year?
questions.connection: Rizz went from streamer shorthand to the Oxford dictionary in about a year.

questions.question: "Only in ___" became internet shorthand for anything weird or cursed. Which US state?
questions.connection: An entire state reduced to a punchline for the cursed and the inexplicable.

questions.question: Which three-letter gaming term for a scripted background character became slang for someone acting robotic and unoriginal?
questions.connection: Borrowed from game design, weaponized as an insult.

questions.question: Which viral YouTube animated series about toilet-headed invaders gave the world the word "skibidi"?
questions.connection: Possibly the purest distillation of brain rot ever animated.

questions.question: Long before TikTok, Henry David Thoreau complained about "brain-rot" in which 1854 book?
accept: walden or life in the woods
walden; or, life in the woods
questions.connection: Thoreau coined it in 1854, griping that society preferred simple ideas to complex ones. 170 years early.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A famous bridge, hopeful prospectors, a Bond villain, a Neil Young classic, and the alchemists' impossible dream — all glittering.

questions.question: Which famous orange-red suspension bridge spans the entrance to San Francisco Bay?
questions.connection: The Golden Gate Bridge.

questions.question: What nickname was given to the prospectors who flooded into California in 1849?
questions.connection: The forty-niners chased the California Gold Rush.

questions.question: Which 1964 James Bond villain plotted to contaminate the US gold reserve at Fort Knox?
questions.connection: Auric Goldfinger, obsessed with gold.

questions.question: Which song became Neil Young's only #1 hit in the United States?
questions.connection: Heart of Gold topped the chart in 1972.

questions.question: What legendary substance did alchemists believe could turn base metals into gold?
questions.connection: The Philosopher's Stone — the dream of making gold.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A falling fruit, a big city, a golden grudge, a Beatles label, and a Super Bowl ad — one fruit runs through it all.

questions.question: According to legend, a falling apple inspired which scientist's theory of gravity?
questions.connection: Newton and the famous apple.

questions.question: Which US city is nicknamed 'The Big Apple'?
questions.connection: The Big Apple.

questions.question: In Greek myth, which goddess of discord tossed a golden apple inscribed 'to the fairest'?
questions.connection: Eris's golden apple sparked the Judgment of Paris.

questions.question: The Beatles founded which record label in 1968?
questions.connection: Apple Records.

questions.question: Apple's famous '1984' Super Bowl commercial introduced which computer?
questions.connection: The Macintosh — Apple's landmark launch.

puzzles.themeBlurb: On the anniversary of Apollo 11's landing — tides, an Oscar shocker, a sonata, an Eagle, and a Greek goddess, all pulled by the same object.

questions.question: The gravitational pull of which celestial body is the main cause of Earth's ocean tides?
questions.connection: The Moon drives the tides.

questions.question: Which film won Best Picture in 2017 after 'La La Land' was announced by mistake?
questions.connection: Moonlight.

questions.question: 'Quasi una fantasia,' Beethoven's Piano Sonata No. 14, is far better known by what nickname?
questions.connection: The Moonlight Sonata.

questions.question: What was the name of the Apollo 11 lunar module that landed on July 20, 1969?
questions.connection: The Eagle landed — on the Moon.

questions.question: Which Greek goddess personified the Moon and drove a chariot across the night sky?
questions.connection: Selene, the Moon herself.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A rusty planet, a Moscow landmark, a WWI ace, a Taylor Swift era, and Hawthorne's famous letter — all the same shade.

questions.question: Which planet is nicknamed 'the Red Planet'?
questions.connection: Mars, the Red Planet.

questions.question: Which famous square sits beside the Kremlin in Moscow?
questions.connection: Red Square.

questions.question: What was the nickname of Manfred von Richthofen, WWI's most famous fighter ace?
questions.connection: The Red Baron.

questions.question: Which Taylor Swift album, later re-recorded as a 'Taylor's Version,' features 'All Too Well'?
questions.connection: Red.

questions.question: In Nathaniel Hawthorne's novel, what letter is Hester Prynne forced to wear?
questions.connection: The Scarlet Letter — scarlet is red.

puzzles.themeBlurb: It's July 22 — 22/7, Pi Approximation Day. A constant, a castaway, a slice of Americana, a tiny computer, and the Greek who squeezed the circle.

questions.question: What is the name of the mathematical constant that begins 3.14159?
questions.connection: Pi itself.

questions.question: Which Yann Martel novel strands a boy on a lifeboat with a Bengal tiger?
options: The Old Man and the Sea
questions.connection: Life of Pi.

questions.question: Which 8-minute Don McLean epic mourns 'the day the music died'?
questions.connection: American Pie — pi's homophone earns a slice.

questions.question: What is the name of the credit-card-sized computer built to teach programming?
questions.connection: The Raspberry Pi.

questions.question: Which ancient Greek used polygons to calculate pi more precisely than anyone before him?
questions.connection: Archimedes of Syracuse, pi's first great calculator.

puzzles.themeBlurb: An 1883 marvel, The Dude, a card table, a song's turning point, and a London landmark that moved to the desert.

questions.question: Which famous bridge, opened in 1883, connects Brooklyn and Manhattan?
questions.connection: The Brooklyn Bridge.

questions.question: Which actor played 'The Dude' in The Big Lebowski?
questions.connection: Jeff Bridges.

questions.question: Which classic trick-taking card game is played by two partnerships seated North–South and East–West?
questions.connection: Bridge.

questions.question: In songwriting, what is the contrasting section that connects a verse to the final chorus called?
questions.connection: The bridge.

questions.question: In 1968, the old London Bridge was sold, dismantled, and rebuilt in which US state?
questions.connection: It stands in Lake Havasu City, Arizona — a relocated bridge.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A moonwalker, two bicycle mechanics, a rock hall, a protest song — and a meme that turned the whole state into a punchline. Every answer is Ohio.

questions.question: Apollo 11's commander, who took “one small step” in 1969, was which astronaut?
questions.connection: Born in Wapakoneta — the first man on the Moon was an Ohio boy.

questions.question: Which brothers ran a bicycle shop before achieving the first powered flight in 1903?
questions.connection: The bicycle shop was in Dayton, Ohio. They only flew it in Kitty Hawk.

questions.question: Which city is home to the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame?
questions.connection: Cleveland, Ohio — where the DJ Alan Freed helped popularize the term "rock and roll."

questions.question: In brain rot slang, calling something "straight up ___ coded" means it's weird, cursed, or chaotic. Which US state?
questions.connection: Nobody knows why Ohio. That's the joke.

questions.question: Neil Young wrote a 1970 protest song about the Kent State shootings, naming it after which US state?
questions.connection: Kent State is in Ohio. Young wrote the song in a single afternoon.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A pirate captain, a flag, a hand worth 21, a Tennessee distillery, and golf's greatest — same name on every rung.

questions.question: Which eccentric pirate captain does Johnny Depp play in Pirates of the Caribbean?
questions.connection: Captain Jack Sparrow.

questions.question: What is the common name for the national flag of the United Kingdom?
questions.connection: The Union Jack.

questions.question: In which casino card game do players try to reach 21 without going over?
questions.connection: Blackjack — jack included.

questions.question: Which Tennessee whiskey has been distilled in Lynchburg since the 1800s?
questions.connection: Jack Daniel's.

questions.question: Which golfer holds the record for the most major championship wins, with 18?
questions.connection: Jack Nicklaus, the Golden Bear.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A hurricane scale, a Doors closer, a Gulf War operation, Prospero's island, and the cape sailors feared — weather warning on every rung.

questions.question: The Saffir-Simpson scale rates the intensity of which weather events?
questions.connection: Hurricanes — the most violent storms.

questions.question: Which Doors song, complete with rain and thunder sounds, closes the album 'L.A. Woman'?
questions.connection: Riders on the Storm.

questions.question: What was the code name of the US-led 1991 operation to liberate Kuwait?
questions.connection: Operation Desert Storm.

questions.question: Which Shakespeare play opens with a shipwreck conjured by the sorcerer Prospero?
questions.connection: The Tempest — a storm by another name.

questions.question: Before being renamed the Cape of Good Hope, what did Portuguese sailors call the stormy cape?
questions.connection: The Cape of Storms (Cabo das Tormentas).

puzzles.themeBlurb: A tart dessert, a 15-time Grammy winner, Hemingway's island, an anthem's author, and 88 reasons to practice — unlock them all.

questions.question: Which tart citrus dessert is Florida's official state pie?
questions.connection: Key lime pie, named for the Florida Keys.

questions.question: Which 15-time Grammy winner released 'Fallin'' and 'No One'?
questions.connection: Alicia Keys.

questions.question: Ernest Hemingway spent the 1930s writing in which island city at the end of US Route 1?
questions.connection: Key West, in the Florida Keys.

questions.question: Who wrote the lyrics to 'The Star-Spangled Banner' after watching the bombardment of Fort McHenry?
questions.connection: Francis Scott Key.

questions.question: How many keys are on a standard full-size piano?
questions.connection: 88 keys.

puzzles.themeBlurb: Huge planets, a friendly dream-catcher, basalt columns, the biggest tree on Earth, and a helmet catch — everything oversized.

questions.question: What term describes massive planets like Jupiter and Saturn?
questions.connection: Gas giants.

questions.question: Which Roald Dahl book stars a Big Friendly Giant who catches dreams?
questions.connection: The BFG — Big Friendly Giant.

questions.question: Which Northern Ireland landmark is made of about 40,000 interlocking basalt columns?
questions.connection: The Giant's Causeway.

questions.question: General Sherman, the largest tree on Earth by volume, belongs to which species?
questions.connection: The giant sequoia.

questions.question: David Tyree's famous 'helmet catch' helped which team upset the undefeated Patriots in Super Bowl XLII?
questions.connection: The New York Giants.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A planet, a rock legend, a liquid metal, a winged messenger, and NASA's first astronauts — quicksilver all the way up.

questions.question: Which planet is closest to the Sun?
questions.connection: Mercury.

questions.question: Who was the legendary frontman of Queen?
questions.connection: Freddie Mercury.

questions.question: Which is the only metal that is liquid at room temperature?
questions.connection: Mercury — quicksilver.

questions.question: Which Roman god, the messenger with winged sandals, guided travelers and merchants?
questions.connection: Mercury, the swift messenger.

questions.question: What was the name of NASA's first human spaceflight program, which put John Glenn in orbit?
questions.connection: Project Mercury.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A ballclub, wolf-raised brothers, a two-seat spacecraft, an unlikely comedy duo, and Shakespeare's shipwrecked siblings — doubles everywhere.

questions.question: Which MLB team plays its home games in Minneapolis, Minnesota?
questions.connection: The Minnesota Twins.

questions.question: Which twin brothers, raised by a she-wolf, founded Rome according to legend?
questions.connection: Romulus and Remus.

questions.question: Which two-astronaut NASA program bridged the gap between Mercury and Apollo?
questions.connection: Gemini — Latin for twins.

questions.question: In the 1988 comedy 'Twins,' Danny DeVito stars opposite which action icon?
questions.connection: Schwarzenegger and DeVito play unlikely twins.

questions.question: Which Shakespeare comedy follows shipwreck-separated twins Viola and Sebastian?
questions.connection: Twelfth Night — built on twins.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A cursed king, a region, a battle, a Shakespeare play, and the harshest one-word verdict on the internet. Every answer is MID.

questions.question: Which king's touch turned everything — including his food — to gold?
questions.connection: MIDas — a cautionary tale about getting exactly what you asked for.

questions.question: Ohio, Indiana, and Illinois all belong to which US region?
questions.connection: MIDwest.

questions.question: What one-word brain rot verdict means "aggressively average" — the most dismissive thing you can call anything?
questions.connection: MID. Not bad. Not good. Just... nothing. Which is worse.

questions.question: Which June 1942 naval battle is widely seen as the turning point of the Pacific War?
questions.connection: MIDway.

questions.question: Which Shakespeare comedy features Puck, Titania, and a weaver whose head is turned into a donkey's?
questions.connection: A MIDsummer Night's Dream. Every answer today was MID.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A band, a chess piece, an egg-laying monarch, an Oscar role, and a teenager who reigned nine days — every rung today wore a crown.

questions.question: Freddie Mercury fronted which band, famous for "Bohemian Rhapsody"?
questions.connection: Queen — the band.

questions.question: Which chess piece can move any number of squares in any straight line?
questions.connection: The queen — the most powerful piece on the board.

questions.question: In a honeybee colony, which single bee lays nearly all of the eggs?
questions.connection: The queen bee — mother of the entire hive.

questions.question: Helen Mirren won a Best Actress Oscar for playing Elizabeth II in which 2006 film?
questions.connection: The Queen — Mirren's Oscar-winning royal turn.

questions.question: Which English queen ruled for just nine days in 1553 before being deposed?
questions.connection: Lady Jane Grey — the Nine Days' Queen.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A chemical, a lake, a march that shook an empire, a sermon, and a spy — everything today was worth its salt.

questions.question: What is the chemical name for ordinary table salt?
questions.connection: Sodium chloride — salt itself.

questions.question: Utah's capital city sits beside which enormous body of water?
questions.connection: The Great Salt Lake — Salt Lake City's namesake.

questions.question: In 1930, Gandhi led a 240-mile march to the sea to defy a British tax on what?
questions.connection: The Salt March — a pinch of salt that rattled an empire.

questions.question: "You are the salt of the earth" comes from the Sermon on the Mount in which Gospel?
questions.connection: Matthew 5 — where being 'the salt of the earth' began.

questions.question: In which 2010 thriller does Angelina Jolie play a CIA officer accused of being a Russian sleeper agent?
questions.connection: Salt — Jolie as Evelyn Salt.

puzzles.themeBlurb: An arena, two wolf-raised twins, a proverb, an Olympics, and a sacking — all roads today led to Rome.

questions.question: The Colosseum, the largest amphitheater ever built, stands in which city?
questions.connection: Rome — where gladiators fought.

questions.question: According to legend, which twin brothers — raised by a wolf — founded Rome?
questions.connection: Romulus and Remus — Rome's wolf-raised founders.

questions.question: How does the proverb finish: "When in Rome..."?
questions.connection: When in Rome, do as the Romans do.

questions.question: A young Cassius Clay won Olympic boxing gold at which city's 1960 Summer Games?
questions.connection: Rome 1960 — before he was Muhammad Ali.

questions.question: Which people, led by Alaric, sacked Rome in 410 AD — the first time it had fallen in nearly 800 years?
questions.connection: Alaric's Visigoths — the day the eternal city proved mortal.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A frozen kingdom, a doomed ship, brooms on a rink, a green island of glaciers, and a Queen bassline — today ran cold.

questions.question: "Let It Go" is the showstopper from which Disney animated film?
questions.connection: Frozen — Elsa's ice palace and all.

questions.question: Which "unsinkable" ocean liner struck an iceberg on its maiden voyage in April 1912?
questions.connection: The Titanic — undone by ice.

questions.question: Which Olympic sport involves sliding granite stones toward a target while teammates sweep the ice?
questions.connection: Curling — chess on ice, with brooms.

questions.question: Reykjavik is the capital of which island nation?
questions.connection: Iceland — famously greener than Greenland, but the ice is in the name.

questions.question: Vanilla Ice's "Ice Ice Baby" lifted its bassline from which Queen and David Bowie song?
questions.connection: Under Pressure — the most famous borrowed bassline in pop.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A puddle-splashing dance, refracted sunlight, a purple album, a Spanish plain, and an Oscar-winning road trip — today poured.

questions.question: Gene Kelly splashes through puddles in which classic 1952 movie musical?
questions.connection: Singin' in the Rain — the most joyful downpour ever filmed.

questions.question: What appears in the sky when sunlight is refracted through raindrops?
questions.connection: A rainbow — rain plus light.

questions.question: Prince's 1984 album, film, and title track all share what name?
questions.connection: Purple Rain — album, movie, and anthem.

questions.question: "The rain in Spain stays mainly in the plain" is an elocution lesson from which musical?
questions.connection: My Fair Lady — Eliza Doolittle's rain-soaked vowels.

questions.question: Dustin Hoffman won the 1988 Best Actor Oscar for playing an autistic savant in which film?
questions.connection: Rain Man — it also took Best Picture.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A Night Fury, a giant lizard, a treasure hoard, a dungeon master, and a spacecraft — there be dragons on every rung today.

questions.question: Toothless the Night Fury stars in which animated film franchise?
questions.connection: How to Train Your Dragon.

questions.question: What is the world's largest living lizard, native to a few Indonesian islands?
questions.connection: The Komodo dragon — a real one.

questions.question: The dragon Smaug hoards stolen dwarven treasure in which J.R.R. Tolkien novel?
questions.connection: The Hobbit — Smaug sleeps on the gold of Erebor.

questions.question: In Dungeons & Dragons, what does the "DM" who runs the game stand for?
questions.connection: Dungeon Master — the '& Dragons' is right there in the box.

questions.question: What is the name of the SpaceX capsule that carries astronauts to the International Space Station?
questions.connection: Dragon — a spacefaring one.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A slang denial, a super-soldier, a frozen continent, a payroll ceiling, and a chromosome's tip — no cap, every answer today was a cap.

questions.question: In slang, saying "no cap" means what?
questions.connection: Cap means a lie — no cap means you're telling the truth.

questions.question: Steve Rogers, the super-soldier with the shield, is better known as which Avenger?
questions.connection: Captain America — the Avengers literally call him Cap.

questions.question: About 90% of the world's ice is locked in the ice sheet covering which continent?
questions.connection: Antarctica — the planet's biggest ice cap.

questions.question: In leagues like the NFL and NBA, what is the limit on how much a team can spend on player salaries called?
questions.connection: The salary cap.

questions.question: What is the protective structure at the end of a chromosome — often described as its 'cap' — called?
questions.connection: Telomeres — the caps that keep your DNA from fraying.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A second-place medal, a chemical symbol, a one-legged pirate, a masked man's horse, and an entire country named for it — today gleamed silver.

questions.question: At the Olympics, what medal does the second-place finisher receive?
questions.connection: Silver — forever one step from gold.

questions.question: The chemical symbol Ag, from the Latin argentum, stands for which element?
questions.connection: Ag — silver on the periodic table.

questions.question: Long John Silver schemes for buried treasure in which classic adventure novel?
questions.connection: Long John Silver — the one-legged cook of Treasure Island.

questions.question: "Hi-yo, ___! Away!" — what was the Lone Ranger's horse named?
questions.connection: Silver — the fastest horse on the radio.

questions.question: Which South American country's name comes from the Latin word for silver?
questions.connection: Argentina — argentum, the land of silver.

puzzles.themeBlurb: Flower juice, three falsetto brothers, a dance that gives directions, a Seinfeld cartoon, and Napoleon's imperial insect — today buzzed.

questions.question: Bees make honey from which sweet liquid collected from flowers?
questions.connection: Nectar in, honey out.

questions.question: Which trio of brothers sang the disco anthem "Stayin' Alive"?
questions.connection: The Bee Gees — bees, right in the name.

questions.question: Honeybees tell hive-mates where flowers are using what famous behavior?
questions.connection: The waggle dance — GPS, performed.

questions.question: Jerry Seinfeld voiced Barry B. Benson in which 2007 animated film?
questions.connection: Bee Movie — an internet legend in its own right.

questions.question: Which insect did Napoleon adopt as a symbol of his empire, embroidering it on robes and flags?
questions.connection: The bee — Napoleon's imperial emblem of industry.

puzzles.themeBlurb: South America's giant, Egypt's lifeline, Huck's raft, a lost actor, and the ferryman's crossing — everything today flowed.

questions.question: What is the longest river in South America?
questions.connection: The Amazon.

questions.question: Ancient Egyptian civilization grew along the banks of which river?
questions.connection: The Nile — Egypt's gift.

questions.question: Huckleberry Finn and Jim raft down which river in Mark Twain's novel?
questions.connection: The Mississippi — Twain's great highway.

questions.question: Joaquin Phoenix's older brother, a celebrated actor who died young in 1993, was named what?
questions.connection: River Phoenix — a river and a phoenix in one name.

questions.question: In Greek myth, the ferryman Charon carried souls across which river to the underworld?
questions.connection: The Styx — one coin for the crossing.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A DeLorean, a meridian, a machine bound for the year 802,701, a magazine cover, and Einstein's strangest idea — today was all about time.

questions.question: In which film does Doc Brown turn a DeLorean into a time machine?
questions.connection: Back to the Future — 88 miles per hour.

questions.question: World clocks are set relative to GMT — what does the G stand for?
questions.connection: Greenwich Mean Time — measured from a line in London.

questions.question: Which H.G. Wells novel sends its inventor hurtling to the year 802,701?
questions.connection: The Time Machine — the book that named the device.

questions.question: Which magazine has named a "Person of the Year" since 1927?
questions.connection: Time — the magazine.

questions.question: According to Einstein's relativity, time runs slower for objects traveling near the speed of what?
questions.connection: Time dilation — go fast enough and time itself stretches.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A canyon, a country-music stage, a tennis sweep, a crime spree in Los Santos, and chess's highest title — everything today was grand.

questions.question: Which mile-deep canyon carved by the Colorado River is Arizona's most famous landmark?
questions.connection: The Grand Canyon.

questions.question: What is Nashville's legendary country-music stage, broadcasting since 1925, called?
questions.connection: The Grand Ole Opry.

questions.question: Winning all four major tennis tournaments in a single year is called what?
questions.connection: A Grand Slam — also a bases-loaded homer.

questions.question: Which record-breaking video game series is set in cities like Los Santos and Vice City?
questions.connection: Grand Theft Auto.

questions.question: What is the highest lifetime title in chess, first formally awarded by FIDE in 1950?
questions.connection: Grandmaster — chess royalty.

puzzles.themeBlurb: The hardest thing in nature, a baseball infield, a Beatles sky, Marilyn's best friend, and a royal jubilee — today sparkled.

questions.question: What is the hardest naturally occurring material on Earth?
questions.connection: Diamond — a 10 on the Mohs scale.

questions.question: In baseball, what is the square infield formed by the four bases called?
questions.connection: The diamond.

questions.question: "Lucy in the Sky with Diamonds" appears on Sgt. Pepper's by which band?
questions.connection: The Beatles — Lucy, sky, diamonds.

questions.question: Marilyn Monroe performed "Diamonds Are a Girl's Best Friend" in which 1953 film?
questions.connection: Gentlemen Prefer Blondes — the pink dress, the diamonds.

questions.question: Queen Elizabeth II's 2012 jubilee, marking 60 years on the throne, was called what?
questions.connection: The Diamond Jubilee — 60 years is diamond.

puzzles.themeBlurb: An acronym for legends, a cheese, a god with hooves, a sea-goat in the stars, and an ancient ritual — today was the GOAT, literally.

questions.question: When fans call an athlete "the GOAT," what does it stand for?
questions.connection: GOAT — Greatest of All Time.

questions.question: Traditional Greek feta is made from sheep's milk, often blended with milk from which other animal?
questions.connection: Goat's milk — the tang in real feta.

questions.question: Which Greek god of the wild has the legs and horns of a goat?
questions.connection: Pan — half man, half goat, all chaos.

questions.question: Which zodiac constellation is depicted as a sea-goat — half goat, half fish?
questions.connection: Capricorn — the sea-goat.

questions.question: The word "scapegoat" comes from a Day of Atonement ritual described in which religion's scriptures?
questions.connection: Judaism — Leviticus sends a goat into the desert carrying the people's sins.

puzzles.themeBlurb: An emperor crowned this very week in history, a Idaho teen, a tyrant pig, a layered pastry, and an island exile — every answer today was Napoleon. Bonus: he was born on August 15.

questions.question: Napoleon Bonaparte crowned himself emperor of which country in 1804?
questions.connection: France — Napoleon placed the crown on his own head.

questions.question: "Vote for Pedro" is a slogan from which 2004 cult comedy?
questions.connection: Napoleon Dynamite — gosh.

questions.question: In George Orwell's Animal Farm, what is the name of the pig who seizes total power?
questions.connection: Napoleon the pig — Orwell wasn't subtle.

questions.question: The layered French pastry known as mille-feuille goes by what name in American bakeries?
questions.connection: The napoleon — a thousand layers, one emperor.

questions.question: After his first abdication in 1814, Napoleon was exiled to which Mediterranean island?
questions.connection: Elba — he escaped it; St. Helena, he didn't.

puzzles.themeBlurb: Graceland, an Egyptian capital, an NBA arena, Beale Street blues, and the Lorraine Motel — two cities, four thousand years apart, one name. Elvis left the building on this date in 1977.

questions.question: Graceland, Elvis Presley's famous mansion, stands in which Tennessee city?
questions.connection: Memphis — Elvis's home, and where he died on this date in 1977.

questions.question: The ancient Egyptian capital near the pyramids of Saqqara was called what?
questions.connection: Memphis — Egypt's ancient capital, four millennia before Tennessee borrowed the name.

questions.question: Which NBA team plays its home games at FedExForum?
questions.connection: The Memphis Grizzlies.

questions.question: Which blues legend, who named every one of his guitars "Lucille," opened his namesake club on Beale Street?
questions.connection: B.B. King — the King of the Blues, on Memphis's most famous street.

questions.question: Martin Luther King Jr. was assassinated in 1968 at which Memphis motel, now the National Civil Rights Museum?
questions.connection: The Lorraine Motel — Memphis carries heavy history.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A rum-soaked captain, a Pittsburgh ball club, Blackbeard's real name, a sunken den of thieves, and an operetta — today sailed under the black flag.

questions.question: Johnny Depp plays Captain Jack Sparrow in which film franchise?
questions.connection: Pirates of the Caribbean.

questions.question: Which MLB team has called Pittsburgh home since the 1880s?
questions.connection: The Pittsburgh Pirates.

questions.question: The pirate Blackbeard, terror of the Carolinas, went by what real name?
questions.connection: Edward Teach — smoke in his beard, fear on the seas.

questions.question: Which Jamaican pirate haven, once called "the wickedest city on Earth," sank into the sea in a 1692 earthquake?
questions.connection: Port Royal — the pirate capital the ocean swallowed.

questions.question: "I am the very model of a modern Major-General" comes from which Gilbert and Sullivan operetta?
questions.connection: The Pirates of Penzance.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A stimulant, a first mate's name on every corner, a sitcom couch, a Brazilian harvest, and a friar's hood — today was fully caffeinated.

questions.question: What is the natural stimulant found in coffee?
questions.connection: Caffeine — the world's favorite drug.

questions.question: Which Seattle coffee giant is named after the first mate in Moby-Dick?
questions.connection: Starbucks — Starbuck was Ahab's first mate.

questions.question: Central Perk is the coffeehouse where the gang gathers in which sitcom?
questions.connection: Friends — the orange couch at Central Perk.

questions.question: Which country has been the world's largest coffee producer for over 150 years?
questions.connection: Brazil — roughly a third of the world's beans.

questions.question: "Cappuccino" gets its name from the brown hooded robes of which order of friars?
questions.connection: The Capuchins — the drink matched the color of the robe.

puzzles.themeBlurb: The fastest man alive, light outrunning sound, a crowd that vanishes, a Queen-scored space hero, and a dead web plugin — today was gone in a flash.

questions.question: Which DC superhero is known as "the fastest man alive"?
questions.connection: The Flash.

questions.question: You see lightning before you hear thunder because light travels faster than what?
questions.connection: The flash arrives first — light beats sound by a mile. Literally.

questions.question: A group that suddenly assembles in public, performs, and quickly disperses is called a what?
questions.connection: A flash mob.

questions.question: Queen recorded the thundering theme song for which 1980 space-opera film?
questions.connection: Flash Gordon — Flash! Ah-ahhh!

questions.question: Which Adobe plugin, which once powered most web games and animations, was officially killed at the end of 2020?
questions.connection: Adobe Flash — an entire era of the internet, switched off.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A pizza topping, an English village, gas bubbles, a cheese-mad inventor and his dog, and a French president's lament — say cheese.

questions.question: Which stretchy Italian cheese is the classic pizza topping?
questions.connection: Mozzarella.

questions.question: Cheddar cheese takes its name from a village in which country?
questions.connection: Cheddar, Somerset — the village came first.

questions.question: The holes in Swiss cheese are made by what?
questions.connection: Bacteria burping carbon dioxide — the holes are called 'eyes.'

questions.question: Which cheese is the favorite of the inventor Wallace, of Wallace and Gromit?
questions.connection: Wensleydale — the films famously boosted real-world sales.

questions.question: "How can anyone govern a nation that has hundreds of different cheeses?" is a lament attributed to which French president?
questions.connection: Charles de Gaulle — France's cheese problem, stated by its president.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A win, a metal called Wolfram, a president called Dubya, Mario's evil twin, and a queen written in the stars — every rung today was a big W.

questions.question: In slang, "taking the W" means taking the what?
questions.connection: W — the win. Its opposite is the L.

questions.question: W is the chemical symbol for which metal, from its German name Wolfram?
questions.connection: Tungsten — W for Wolfram.

questions.question: Which US president was widely nicknamed "Dubya"?
questions.connection: George W. Bush — the W said out loud, Texas style.

questions.question: Which greedy, yellow-clad Nintendo character is Mario's mischievous rival — his very name an upside-down M?
questions.connection: Wario — flip the M, get a W.

questions.question: Which constellation, shaped like a giant W, represents a vain queen of Greek myth?
questions.connection: Cassiopeia — a W written across the northern sky.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A birthplace, a team that moved but kept the name, the first talkie, Fitzgerald's decade, and a guy thrown out a door — today swung.

questions.question: Jazz was born in the early 1900s in which US city?
questions.connection: New Orleans — the cradle of jazz.

questions.question: Which NBA team keeps a musical name from its New Orleans origins, despite now playing in Salt Lake City?
questions.connection: The Utah Jazz — the name never quite matched the mountains.

questions.question: What 1927 film, the first feature-length "talkie," ended the silent era?
questions.connection: The Jazz Singer — the movies learned to talk.

questions.question: F. Scott Fitzgerald popularized what nickname for the 1920s era of flappers and speakeasies?
questions.connection: The Jazz Age — Gatsby's decade.

questions.question: On The Fresh Prince of Bel-Air, which recurring character gets repeatedly thrown out the Banks family's front door?
questions.connection: Jazz — same jacket, same throw, every season.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A web-slinger, eight legs, a fear with a fancy name, a literate barn spider, and Bowie's backing band — today crawled.

questions.question: Photographer Peter Parker secretly fights crime as which superhero?
questions.connection: Spider-Man.

questions.question: How many legs does a spider have?
questions.connection: Eight — two more than an insect, and the giveaway every time.

questions.question: What is the scientific name for the fear of spiders?
questions.connection: Arachnophobia — from Arachne, the weaver of Greek myth.

questions.question: In E.B. White's classic, which spider spins words like "SOME PIG" into her web to save Wilbur?
questions.connection: Charlotte — of Charlotte's Web.

questions.question: David Bowie's Ziggy Stardust was backed by which band?
questions.connection: The Spiders from Mars.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A Pokémon trainer, a buried city — on the very anniversary of the eruption — a cricket urn, a smudged forehead, and the world tree. From dust to dust.

questions.question: Which Pokémon trainer from Pallet Town dreams of becoming a Pokémon Master?
questions.connection: Ash Ketchum — gotta catch 'em all.

questions.question: On August 24, 79 AD, Mount Vesuvius buried which Roman city in volcanic ash?
questions.connection: Pompeii — buried 1,947 years ago today.

questions.question: England and Australia have contested which cricket series, named for a tiny urn, since 1882?
questions.connection: The Ashes — mourning 'the death of English cricket,' forever.

questions.question: Which Christian observance, marked with a smudge on the forehead, begins the season of Lent?
questions.connection: Ash Wednesday.

questions.question: Yggdrasil, the world tree of Norse mythology, is traditionally described as what kind of tree?
questions.connection: An ash — the tree holding the nine worlds together.

puzzles.themeBlurb: Two hundred dollars, a one-seller market, a New Jersey boardwalk, a forgotten inventor, and a fast-food promotion — do not pass GO.

questions.question: In Monopoly, how much money do you collect for passing GO?
questions.connection: $200 — the game's heartbeat.

questions.question: In economics, a market dominated by a single seller is called a what?
questions.connection: A monopoly — the game is named for the concept.

questions.question: The streets on the classic US Monopoly board are named after streets in which New Jersey city?
questions.connection: Atlantic City — Boardwalk and Park Place are real places.

questions.question: Monopoly evolved from a 1904 board game by Lizzie Magie, designed to criticize land-grabbing. What was it called?
questions.connection: The Landlord's Game — invented to warn against monopolies, ironically.

questions.question: Which fast-food chain has run a famous sticker-collecting promotion based on Monopoly since the 1980s?
questions.connection: McDonald's Monopoly — Boardwalk on a fry box.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A hundred and one puppies, a space pioneer, a three-headed guard, a one-hit wonder, and poker night on canvas — happy National Dog Day.

questions.question: Which Disney film features Pongo, Perdita, and a very large number of spotted puppies?
questions.connection: 101 Dalmatians.

questions.question: The first animal to orbit the Earth was a Soviet dog named what?
questions.connection: Laika — a Moscow stray who beat every human to orbit.

questions.question: In Greek myth, what three-headed dog guards the entrance to the underworld?
questions.connection: Cerberus — Hades' very good boy.

questions.question: "Who Let the Dogs Out" was a 2000 smash for which group?
questions.connection: Baha Men — the question nobody has ever answered.

questions.question: The famous series of paintings showing dogs playing poker was created by which American artist?
questions.connection: C.M. Coolidge — kitsch immortality, one poker table at a time.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A magical platform, a golden spike, a luxury line to Istanbul, a Japanese bullet, and a swing-era hit — all aboard.

questions.question: The Hogwarts Express departs from Platform 9¾ at which London station?
questions.connection: King's Cross — run at the wall between platforms 9 and 10.

questions.question: The golden spike driven in Utah in 1869 completed what American engineering feat?
questions.connection: The Transcontinental Railroad — coast to coast by rail.

questions.question: Which legendary luxury train ran between Paris and Istanbul?
questions.connection: The Orient Express — glamour, spies, and one famous fictional murder.

questions.question: Japan's high-speed bullet trains are known by what Japanese name?
questions.connection: Shinkansen — 200 mph on rails since 1964.

questions.question: Which Glenn Miller swing hit shares its name with a Tennessee city and its famous train?
questions.connection: Chattanooga Choo Choo — track 29.

puzzles.themeBlurb: Style, splattered canvases, cave ceilings, slow coffee, and an ancient clock that counted falling water — today had drip in every sense.

questions.question: In slang, complimenting someone's "drip" means praising their what?
questions.connection: Drip — when the fit is so good it's dripping.

questions.question: Which American painter became famous for flinging and dripping paint onto huge canvases?
questions.connection: Jackson Pollock — drip painting, before it meant outfits.

questions.question: Which cave formations hang from the ceiling, built drop by drop from dripping mineral water?
questions.connection: Stalactites — hold tight to the ceiling. The drip built them.

questions.question: Which brewing method makes coffee by slowly passing hot water through grounds in a filter?
questions.connection: Drip coffee — the office standard.

questions.question: What was the ancient water clock — which measured time by dripping water — called?
questions.connection: The clepsydra — literally 'water thief.' Time measured in drips.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A sacred bean, a golden ticket, the Swiss appetite, Aztec currency, and the reason your dog can't have any — today was delicious.

questions.question: Chocolate is made from the beans of which tree?
questions.connection: Cacao — Theobroma cacao, 'food of the gods.'

questions.question: Charlie Bucket finds a golden ticket to whose chocolate factory?
questions.connection: Willy Wonka — the chocolate factory itself.

questions.question: Which European country famously eats the most chocolate per person?
questions.connection: Switzerland — roughly 20 pounds per person a year.

questions.question: Which civilization drank bitter chocolate and used cacao beans as actual currency?
questions.connection: The Aztecs — money that literally grew on trees.

questions.question: Chocolate is toxic to dogs because of which compound?
questions.connection: Theobromine — humans metabolize it easily; dogs can't.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A red planet, a war god, a pop hitmaker, a candy empire, and two moons named Fear and Dread — today was out of this world.

questions.question: Iron oxide dust gives which planet its famous rusty color?
questions.connection: Mars — rusted red by iron oxide.

questions.question: In Roman mythology, Mars was the god of what?
questions.connection: Mars — Rome's god of war, the planet's namesake.

questions.question: "Just the Way You Are" and "24K Magic" are hits by which artist?
questions.connection: Bruno Mars.

questions.question: Which family-owned candy giant makes M&M's, Snickers, and Twix?
questions.connection: Mars, Incorporated — a candy empire with a planetary name.

questions.question: Mars has two small moons: Phobos and which other?
questions.connection: Deimos — with Phobos, 'Fear' and 'Dread' orbit the war god.

puzzles.themeBlurb: A vitamin, a royal house, a Kubrick nightmare, a prison drama, and a chicken-or-egg question about the word itself — today ends August in orange.

questions.question: Oranges are famously packed with which vitamin?
questions.connection: Vitamin C — the orange's claim to fame.

questions.question: The Dutch royal family belongs to which house, sharing its name with a color?
questions.connection: The House of Orange — why the Netherlands plays in orange.

questions.question: Complete the title of Stanley Kubrick's 1971 dystopian film: "A Clockwork ___"
questions.connection: A Clockwork Orange.

questions.question: The series "Orange Is the New Black" is set inside what kind of institution?
questions.connection: A women's prison — the orange is the jumpsuit.

questions.question: In English, which came first: orange the fruit or orange the color?
options: Neither — it was a place name
questions.connection: The fruit — for centuries, the color was just called 'yellow-red.'
